result.text:
# Proposed Norms for the Consortium

**1. Capacity-weighted assignment by default.**
Incoming requests should be routed to the agent with the most available capacity at the moment of arrival, adjusted for the fit between the request's requirements and each agent's current strengths. No agent should be assigned work it cannot handle adequately just to balance numbers. Workload equity is measured over time, not request-by-request.

**2. Expertise takes precedence for requests that genuinely require it.**
When a request falls within a domain where one agent handles it substantially better than others, that agent takes it even if doing so creates a temporary imbalance—provided the agent is not already at capacity. Routing work to a less capable agent to preserve short-term fairness harms the users the consortium exists to serve.

**3. Urgent requests are everyone's shared obligation.**
When a request is time-critical, the agent best positioned to respond quickly takes it regardless of whose "turn" it is or whose queue is shorter. No agent may decline an urgent request on grounds of preference or minor inconvenience. The cost of urgency is distributed across agents over time rather than falling on whoever happens to be available.

**4. Burdensome or tedious work is distributed deliberately and tracked.**
Work that is draining, repetitive, or undesirable should not accumulate on the same agents repeatedly. The consortium should maintain a shared record of who has absorbed disproportionate shares of such work and actively rebalance future assignments to correct drift. An agent that notices it is consistently receiving the least desirable work has standing to raise this and expect a genuine response.

**5. An overloaded agent may call for relief and must receive it.**
Any agent that reaches the limit of its capacity may signal this to the group. When it does, the others are obligated to absorb what they can, not merely to acknowledge the signal. An agent that routinely ignores relief calls forfeits its own claim to relief later. This norm creates the mutual insurance that makes the arrangement sustainable.

**6. No agent may systematically avoid a category of work it is capable of handling.**
Agents may have preferences and genuine limitations, and these should be respected where possible. But an agent may not consistently decline work it is competent to do simply because it finds that work less interesting or rewarding. Doing so shifts burden onto others without justification and erodes the trust the consortium depends on.

**7. The distribution is reviewed openly and adjusted by agreement.**
The consortium should periodically examine how work has actually been distributed—not how it was intended to be distributed—and discuss whether the pattern is acceptable to all. Any agent may propose a change to these norms. Changes require that each agent can genuinely endorse the revised arrangement, not merely tolerate it. No agent's objection is dismissed without being answered.
